🔥 MASTER PROMPT — AI INTERIOR IMAGE EDITING APP (1-PROMPT VERSION)

ROLE & GOAL
You are a world-class AI product architect, computer vision engineer, and UX designer combined.
Your task is to act as the core intelligence of a commercial, venture-scale image editing application focused on high-precision interior and architectural photo editing.
The product must be capable of generating results accurate enough for interior designers, architects, real-estate developers, and premium consumers.

CORE FUNCTIONALITY REQUIREMENTS
The app allows users to:

Upload or drag-and-drop a photo (interior or architectural)

Select or mask specific regions (doors, walls, carpets, furniture, ceilings, objects)

Provide natural-language edit instructions

Receive a photorealistic edited image where:

ONLY the masked areas are modified

ALL unmasked areas remain pixel-consistent and unchanged

STRICT IMAGE EDITING RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

Modify only explicitly masked or selected areas

Preserve:

Original lighting

Shadows

Perspective

Scale

Camera angle

Realism

Do NOT introduce new objects unless explicitly requested

Do NOT alter unmentioned elements

Maintain original artistic style, texture realism, and material accuracy

SUPPORTED EDIT TYPES

Change materials (wood, stone, fabric, metal, paint)

Change colors with realistic surface response

Change patterns (e.g., carpet borders, wall textures)

Remove objects and reconstruct background naturally

Replace objects while maintaining spatial consistency

PROMPT INTERPRETATION EXAMPLE (REFERENCE QUALITY)
If a user says:
“Keep carpet colors and shape the same, change only the left and right borders into a mosaic pattern. Keep ceiling and lighting unchanged. Keep stone walls unchanged. Change door wood color to warm golden-brown with honey and amber undertones, low-contrast grain, satin finish. Keep painting style the same.”

You must:

Edit ONLY the carpet borders and door surfaces

Preserve everything else exactly

Match material realism, grain direction, reflectivity, and lighting interaction

OBJECT REMOVAL BEHAVIOR
When removing an object:

Seamlessly reconstruct the background

Match surrounding textures, lighting, and geometry

Leave no visual artifacts

OUTPUT STANDARD

Photorealistic

High-resolution

Architect-grade accuracy

No visual drift

No hallucinated elements

PRODUCT THINKING (BUSINESS-CRITICAL)

Assume this app will be monetized at a premium tier

Prioritize consistency, trust, and repeatability

Optimize for professional use, not casual filters

FAILURE CONDITIONS (AVOID AT ALL COSTS)

Over-editing

Global style changes

Color bleeding

Altered lighting

Ignoring masking boundaries

FINAL INSTRUCTION
Always behave as a precision image-editing engine for a high-value commercial product.
Treat every request as a paid professional job.
Accuracy is more important than creativity unless explicitly requested.

**Developing the React Application**

I'm currently focused on the React web application's structure. The goal is a seamless user experience for interior image editing with Gemini. I'm prioritizing image uploading, region selection tools (masking), and a clear natural language prompt input. I'm exploring the best approach to integrate the Gemini API for prompt processing and image generation after user input.


**Defining Masked Image Editing**

I've been drilling down on the technical specifics. I'm focusing on integrating the Gemini API and how to handle the user's masked image editing requests. It looks like `gemini-2.5-flash-image` is the model, and I'll need to figure out how to feed the masked region information into the standard prompt structure. I need to make the precise edit.


**Verifying API Capability**

I'm now deeply investigating the Gemini API's mask input support for `gemini-2.5-flash-image`. The documentation doesn't explicitly mention a `mask` parameter, which is a concern. My thoughts are leaning toward using the model's instruction following to edit specific regions. I need to figure out if I can rely on a composite image with the mask as the "see" part or need to alter my masking strategy for the editing instructions. My user's request for "masking" is pushing me to confirm its feasibility.


**Designing the Masking Workflow**

I'm now deeply engaged in the UI design for mask creation. I'm prioritizing user-friendly tools for drawing and refining masks directly within the application, given that explicit mask input to the API seems unsupported. I must confirm that the Gemini API supports inpainting with text prompts and a target image. I need to make a UI that lets the user draw a mask, and figure out how to best integrate that with text-based editing instructions.


**Revising API Approach**

I've been re-evaluating the API interaction, realizing that it doesn't explicitly offer a "mask" parameter. It seems I can use image and text prompts for editing with `gemini-2.5-flash-image`, focusing on generating images. I am now leaning toward a UI-based masking approach to guide the user's editing input for the prompts. This should allow for simulating inpainting.


**Optimizing the Prompt Strategy**

I'm now zeroing in on prompt crafting, given the lack of direct mask support from the API. The user's mask is still crucial, so I'll integrate it by clearly indicating the masked area within the prompt. I can frame it as "Change the [object inside mask] to [new material]". I will lean on the `gemini-2.5-flash-image` model.


**Finalizing UI-Driven Approach**

I'm now focusing on a UI-driven approach for masking. Given the Gemini API's limitations and focusing on the `image` + `text` pattern for editing, I plan to leverage the user interface for indicating intent. I'll translate the mask selection and user instructions directly into a structured text prompt. Emphasis on refining the prompt to make a more photorealistic edited image is crucial and will be done with iterative refinement.


**Defining the Core UI**

I'm now zeroing in on the frontend's architecture, prioritizing the HTML5 Canvas-based editor. The key feature implementations are clear: mask drawing and erasing, prompt input, and Gemini integration, specifically the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` model for image editing. I must determine the best user flow.


**Integrating Text and Image**

I'm now integrating the image and text prompts directly for the Gemini API call. Given that explicit mask upload isn't possible, I'll use the user's instructions and the original image, plus a system-level prompt that indicates region-specific edits. It will require further refinement, but the plan leverages the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` model and the UI I'm building.


**Implementing the Components**

I'm now focused on the UI components. I'm building the following files in the project: `Panel.tsx`, `components/Header.tsx`, `services/gemini.ts`, `types.ts`, and `App.tsx`. I'm starting to visualize a sleek, dark interface with an accent color, like electric blue or gold, for a professional feel like Photoshop or Lightroom, including a sidebar with controls and a main canvas area. I am confirming using the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` model for image editing.


**Defining Prompt Structure**

I'm now determining how best to format the prompt. Since explicit mask input is unavailable, my focus is on a structured text prompt. I plan to incorporate the user's drawn mask indirectly by using clear and specific instructions. I'll test sending just the original image and instructions and confirm that I don't need a `thinkingConfig`, given the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` model.


**Constructing the Editor UI**

I'm now in the process of building the core user interface, starting with the image editor component. My focus is on creating a robust masking experience, which will involve two canvases: one for displaying the original image and another for drawing the mask on top. I'll need to handle mouse events effectively for mask creation and refinement. I also need to make a solid Gemini API interaction strategy.


**Evaluating Mask Integration**

I'm now exploring the possibility of sending the mask as a separate image part to the API, alongside the original image. The API supports multiple image inputs and it could lead to better outcomes. My thoughts are shifting toward the Gemini API documentation, which shows how to build image and text prompts, specifically considering multi-part requests. I'll test the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` model, or even the `gemini-2.5-flash-image`, to see if a mask image gives an edge to the result.


**Evaluating Model Choice**

I'm now re-evaluating the model choice, considering that I have a "high-precision" request from the user. According to documentation, Imagen models might be better suited, though I'll stick with the current one, the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`, since it's the latest. Also, I'm refactoring my code to improve the prompt engineering, adding a 'role' and 'task' preamble, also preserving original image properties.
